texto: Poetry is as necessary to comprehension as science. It is as impossible to live without reverence as it is without joy.

texto: The world to-day is sick to its thin blood for lack of elemental things, for fire before the hands, for water welling from the earth, for air, for the dear earth itself underfoot. In my world of beach and dunes these elemental presences lived and had their being, and under their arch there moved an incomparable pageant of nature and the year.

texto: Learn to reverence night and to put away the vulgar fear of it, for, with the banishment of night from the experience of man, there vanishes as well a religious emotion, a poetic mood, which gives depth to the adventure of humanity. By day, space is one with the earth and with man--it is his sun that is shining, his clouds that are floating past; at night, space is his no more.

texto: And what of Nature itself, you say – that callous and cruel engine, red in tooth and fang? Well, it is not so much of an engine as you think. As for "red in tooth and fang," whenever I hear the phrase or its intellectual echoes I know that some passer-by has been getting life from books.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Henry Beston que provoca limites e escolhas conscientes. Ao questionar “And what of Nature itself, you say – that callous and cruel engine, red in tooth and fang? Well, …”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, nature, engine, com foco em reflexao.

texto: Hold your hands out over the earth as over a flame. To all who love her, who open to her the doors of their veins, she gives of her strength, sustaining them with her own measureless tremor of dark life. Touch the earth, love the earth, honour the earth, her plains, her valleys, her hills, and her seas; rest your spirit in her solitary places. For the gifts of life are the earth’s and they are given to all, and they are the songs of birds at daybreak, Orion and the Bear, and dawn seen over ocean from the beach.
explicacao: Citação de Henry Beston sobre vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. “Hold your hands out over the earth as over a flame. texto: We lose a great deal, I think, when we lose this sense and feeling for the sun. When all has been said, the adventure of the sun is the great natural drama by which we live, and not to have joy in it and awe of it, not to share in it, is to close a dull door on natures's sustaining and poetic spirit.

texto: The three great elemental sounds in nature are the sound of rain, the sound of wind in a primeval wood, and the sound of outer ocean on a beach. I have heard them all, and of the three elemental voices, that of ocean is the most awesome, beautiful and varied.

texto: I muse again on the dogmatic assertion which I often make that the countryman's relation to Nature must never be anything else but an alliance... When we begin to consider Nature as something to be robbed greedily like an unguarded treasure, or used as an enemy, we put ourselves in thought outside of Nature, of which we are inescapably a part.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Henry Beston: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de limites e escolhas conscientes. O trecho “I muse again on the dogmatic assertion which I often make that the countryman's relation to Natur…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, nature, which pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de reflexao.
